findings.title: Hardcoded Vault Passphrase
findings.description: The LUKS vault is unlocked by a passphrase embedded in hardware_lock.py and derived from the Raspberry Pi serial number.
findings.evidence: hardware_lock.py revealed AUTHORIZED_PI_SERIAL = "ffb6d42807368154" and .bash_history contained the exact echo command used for decryption.
findings.impact: Any attacker with local access can decrypt vault.img and access player-server backups, logs, and database snapshots.
findings.remediation: Move key material off device, prompt for passphrase entry on device console, avoid storing credentials in source code, and rotate passphrase per device.
findings.hardening: Use TPM or secure enclave to seal keys to hardware, require two-factor confirmation for decryption, and monitor /usr/local/bin for unauthorized modifications.

findings.title: Newline-Sensitive Passphrase Flow
findings.description: Vault unlock requires `echo "passphrase" | sudo cryptsetup open ...` and fails if the newline is omitted, revealing fragile automation. Attackers could replicate this flow with minimal tools.
findings.evidence: Attempts using `echo -n` failed while exact recorded command worked; script uses `echo` with newline when piping into cryptsetup.
findings.impact: A local user or script with sudo privileges can decrypt vault.img instantly; the automation detail lowers the barrier for replication.
findings.remediation: Use key files or protected key slots instead of echoing passphrases, and remove super-user scripting around sensitive operations.
findings.hardening: Implement passphrase validation with key files, store secrets in encrypted keystore, and audit sudo scripts for unsafe echo usage.

findings.title: Device-Bound Vault Key Exposure
findings.description: The device unlock key relies solely on the Raspberry Pi serial number which is public, so any cloned player can decrypt other vaults if the scripts remain unchanged.
findings.evidence: unlock_vault.py and hardware_lock.py derive the passphrase simply from /proc/cpuinfo serial; Serial `ffb6d42807368154` decoded to the passphrase used for recovery.
findings.impact: Possessing a single authorized Pi or a buildable clone allows decrypting any vault produced by the same factory script.
findings.remediation: Use per-device key material stored securely, incorporate tamper detection, and enforce hardware unique unlocking (TPM / HSM).
findings.hardening: Add code signing for unlock scripts, enforce encrypted key retrieval from a secure service that binds to device identity, and monitor for unauthorized copies in deployment.

enhancements.recommendation: Store vault passphrases in dedicated KMS/TPM hardware instead of plaintext scripts, and rotate keys using authorized deployments only.
Limit which accounts can execute cryptsetup and remove convenience sudo entries from scripts to prevent misuse by less-privileged users.
